target_r1_desc: Angle locked at 45°. Calculate velocity to hit 80m.
target_r2_desc: Velocity locked at 35 m/s. Calculate angle to hit 120m.
target_r3_desc: Angle locked at 30°. Hit the 60m crate.
target_r5_desc: On the Moon! Angle locked at 55°. Hit 100m.
target_r6_desc: Two targets! Angle locked at 40°. Hit both.
target_r7_desc: Air drag ON! Angle locked at 35°. Compensate!
target_r8_desc: Mars gravity. Velocity locked at 45 m/s. Hit 200m.
target_r9_desc: Triple targets! Same angle, adjust velocity each shot.
target_r10_desc: Final challenge: drag ON, velocity locked at 38 m/s. Prove your mastery.
next_predict_then_launch: Predict the range, then launch once and compare the result.
next_turn_on_vectors: Turn on vectors or energy and explain what changes during flight.
next_one_controlled_change: Use one controlled change to improve your next target attempt.
next_change_only_one: Change only angle, velocity, or gravity and compare the new trajectory.
tool_intro_blurb: Investigate how launch conditions shape motion, then support each claim with trajectory evidence.
aria_canvas: Physics projectile simulator — use arrow keys to adjust angle and velocity, Space to launch
aria_show_work_currently: Show your work formulas panel, currently
aria_motion_currently: Motion component graphs (Vx vs t and Vy vs t), currently
aria_step_frame: Step one frame forward (only useful when paused)
aria_symmetry_demo: Symmetry demo: fire current angle, then the complementary angle, at the same velocity
air_drag_warning: Air drag is ON. Actual values will be lower than these no-drag predictions.
launch_to_see_vx_vy: Launch a projectile to see Vx-vs-t and Vy-vs-t.
aria_vx_graph: Horizontal velocity over time, a flat line
vx_flat_note: Flat → no horizontal force, Vx is constant.
aria_vy_graph: Vertical velocity over time, decreasing through zero at apex
vy_line_note: Straight line → constant gravity. Crosses zero at apex.
motion_independent_note: Horizontal and vertical motion are independent. Gravity only affects Vy.
aria_range_angle_graph: Range as a function of launch angle, peaking at 45 degrees
sweep_note_drag: With drag, the actual optimum is lower than 45° (~38-42°). This chart is no-drag.
sweep_note_nodrag: Range peaks at exactly 45° without drag. Complementary angles (e.g. 30° and 60°) hit the same R.
concept_projectile_body: An object launched into the air follows a parabolic path. Horizontal velocity (Vx) stays constant while vertical velocity (Vy) changes due to gravity. Enable Vectors to see!
concept_newton2_body: F = ma. The only force on a projectile (ignoring drag) is gravity: a = g downward. This creates the curved trajectory.
concept_optimal_body: Without air resistance, 45° gives maximum range. With drag, the optimal angle shifts lower (~38-42°). Try it!
concept_energy_body: Kinetic Energy (KE = ½mv²) converts to Potential Energy (PE = mgh) and back. Enable the Energy bar to watch this live!
concept_air_body: Drag force opposes motion and increases with speed (F_drag ∝ v²). It shortens range, lowers max height, and makes the trajectory asymmetric.
concept_gravity_body: Different planets have different gravitational pull. Moon (1.6 m/s²) lets projectiles fly 6x farther than Earth (9.8 m/s²)! Try Jupiter for a challenge.
myth1_label: Myth: “A moving object needs a forward push.”
myth1_body: Watch Vx — it never changes, and the Vectors view shows NO horizontal arrow. With no sideways force, the object keeps moving sideways on its own (Newton's 1st Law).
myth2_body: Drag the Mass slider — the trajectory does NOT change. In a vacuum every mass falls with the same g; mass only scales the energy bar, never the path.
myth3_label: Myth: “Velocity points where the force points.”
myth3_body: At the very top of the arc the velocity is purely horizontal, yet gravity still points straight DOWN. A force changes motion — it doesn't have to point along it.
launch_to_see_flight_data: Launch a projectile to see flight data
lock_angle_suffix: ° — adjust velocity or gravity to hit the target
lock_velocity_suffix: m/s — adjust angle or gravity to hit the target
air_drag_modifies: Air drag modifies these equations — real range will be shorter than the idealized calculation below.
myth35_1_s: A heavier cannonball falls faster, so it lands sooner.
myth35_1_why: Gravity speeds up EVERY mass equally (with air resistance off) — a 10 kg ball and a 1 kg ball trace the exact same arc. Galileo’s big idea.
myth35_1_tryit: Launch with mass = 1 kg, then slide mass to 10 kg and launch again. Same arc, same landing spot.
myth35_2_s: At the very top of the arc, the ball has stopped moving.
myth35_2_why: Only the UP-DOWN part of the motion pauses at the top. The ball keeps moving forward the whole time.
myth35_2_tryit: Turn on Vectors and watch the horizontal arrow at the top of the arc — it never shrinks.
myth35_3_s: Doubling the launch speed doubles the distance.
myth35_3_why: Distance grows with speed × speed. Double the speed and it lands FOUR times farther.
myth35_3_tryit: Launch at 15 m/s, then at 30 m/s with the same angle — compare the landing markers.
myth35_4_s: Aiming at 45° throws the farthest (no air).
myth35_4_why: Lower angles fly flat but fall too soon; higher angles waste speed going up. 45° is the perfect trade.
myth35_4_tryit: Run the complementary-angles demo — 30° and 60° even land on the SAME spot.
myth68_1_s: After launch, a force keeps pushing the ball forward.
myth68_1_why: Once it leaves the cannon, the ONLY force is gravity, pulling straight down. Forward motion continues because nothing stops it — Newton’s first law.
myth68_1_tryit: Watch the vector overlay in flight: there is no forward force, yet Vx stays perfectly constant.
myth68_2_s: A ball fired horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height hit the ground at the same time.
myth68_2_why: Horizontal and vertical motion are independent. Both balls fall with the same gravity from the same height, so they land together.
myth68_2_tryit: Fire at a very low angle and compare the flight time with a steep, short lob from the same height.
myth912_1_s: With air resistance ON, 45° is still the best angle.
myth912_1_why: Drag punishes long, high flights — it bleeds v² the whole way. The optimum drops to roughly 30–40° depending on speed.
myth912_1_tryit: Toggle air resistance on and sweep the angle slider — watch where the landing marker actually peaks.
iq_intro: Three sliders control gravity (any planet), launch angle, and velocity. The simulator tells you whether your projectile range is near-optimal, reasonable, or far-off — a discrete 3-state outcome (no numeric score). Sweep the sliders. Log observations. Type what you discover.
iq_range_mid: m (max possible at this v + g:
